content:
9 min read
Just now

--

The retrieval misses that cluster around our most structured documents are not random. They trace back to a quiet assumption almost every pipeline makes: that a document is a sequence of pages. It is not, and that mismatch is capping our accuracy.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Split tables with connector linesA user asks a finance assistant a simple question: what was the revenue figure for the third region in the quarterly breakdown? The table is right there in the report. The model returns a confident number, but tt may be not right.
Not because the model is weak, and not because retrieval failed to find the table. It found half of it. The table started near the bottom of one page and continued on the next, the parser emitted two separate tables, the chunker split them into two unrelated chunks, and retrieval surfaced the half that had the row labels but not the half with the third region’s number.
The model did what models do with partial evidence: it answered anyway.
If you hit the paywall, use the friend link here: read the full post.
This is not an exotic failure. It is one of the most common ways production RAG quietly degrades, and it has a specific cause.
Almost every RAG pipeline treats a document as a sequence of pages, because that is how the file is stored and…
